MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against sensible, relevant baselines (Baroni et al. 2012 concat and the Shwartz & Dagan PPDB baseline), includes Context2Vec variants, and evaluates on the same datasets and metrics (weighted/Macro-F1) so comparisons appear largely fair. However, from a theory-first perspective key omissions weaken the comparative claims: there is no systematic ablation isolating the contribution of the mask statistics versus classifier capacity (they only report a single logistic regression with no hyperparameter tuning), and it is unclear whether stronger contemporary contextual baselines (e.g. richer non-linear classifiers or recent contextual embedding methods) were tested. The paper does acknowledge some prior methods’ performance and reports alternatives (averaging, other masks), but the lack of classifier and hyperparameter ablations prevents full confidence that gains stem from the proposed features rather than model/optimization differences. | B(4): The paper compares to sensible and relevant baselines (context-agnostic concatenation from Baroni et al. 2012 and a PPDB-specific baseline from Shwartz & Dagan 2015) on the same new datasets and reports standard metrics (weighted/Macro F1), so comparisons are generally fair. It also provides analyses contrasting the masked contextualization with simple averaging and examines Context2Vec behavior, and the related-work coverage is described as thorough. My main reservation is the apparent absence of stronger modern contextual baselines (e.g., neural context-sensitive encoders) and no hyperparameter reporting, which are notable omissions but do not invalidate the empirical comparisons presented.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible, relevant baselines (Baroni et al. 2012 concat and the Shwartz & Dagan PPDB baseline), includes Context2Vec variants, and evaluates on the same datasets and metrics (weighted/Macro-F1) so comparisons appear largely fair. However, from a theory-first perspective key omissions weaken the comparative claims: there is no systematic ablation isolating the contribution of the mask statistics versus classifier capacity (they only report a single logistic regression with no hyperparameter tuning), and it is unclear whether stronger contemporary contextual baselines (e.g. richer non-linear classifiers or recent contextual embedding methods) were tested. The paper does acknowledge some prior methods’ performance and reports alternatives (averaging, other masks), but the lack of classifier and hyperparameter ablations prevents full confidence that gains stem from the proposed features rather than model/optimization differences.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to sensible and relevant baselines (context-agnostic concatenation from Baroni et al. 2012 and a PPDB-specific baseline from Shwartz & Dagan 2015) on the same new datasets and reports standard metrics (weighted/Macro F1), so comparisons are generally fair. It also provides analyses contrasting the masked contextualization with simple averaging and examines Context2Vec behavior, and the related-work coverage is described as thorough. My main reservation is the apparent absence of stronger modern contextual baselines (e.g., neural context-sensitive encoders) and no hyperparameter reporting, which are notable omissions but do not invalidate the empirical comparisons presented.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical results support the main claims that contextualized masked representations and similarity features improve over baselines, but they also both note important omissions (no ablation separating mask statistics from classifier capacity, missing hyperparameter/detail reporting, statistical testing, and limited reproducibility). These shortcomings limit the study’s rigor and interpretability but do not invalidate the empirical findings, so a score slightly above the midpoint is appropriate. I therefore give a 4 to reflect solid empirical support tempered by notable methodological and reporting gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states empirical claims and provides experiments showing gains from contextualized features and a masked filtering method, with comparisons to context-agnostic and prior PPDB baselines and new datasets. However, there is no formal theoretical motivation connecting the mask/statistics to an account of lexical entailment (risking conflating distributional similarity with logical entailment), and the summary lacks critical ablation analyses that isolate the contributions of mask statistics versus classifier capacity. Experimental reporting also omits hyperparameter details and statistical significance testing, and no mathematical derivations are needed or provided. These issues raise substantive concerns but do not fully invalidate the empirical findings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported by empirical evidence: experiments show contextualized masked representations and word-context similarity features improve over context-agnostic baselines and outperform prior PPDB-specific features, and new CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN datasets enable controlled evaluation. There are no formal derivations to check, and the methodology uses reasonable baselines and metrics, but the report omits hyperparameter details, code, and statistical significance testing, and relies on a single simple classifier without ablation of alternative learning models. These are minor technical shortcomings that do not overturn the empirical findings, but they limit reproducibility and leave some claims about generalization and method optimality less firmly established.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem, releases valuable datasets, and demonstrates practical empirical gains (including cross-lingual generalization) that make the work likely to be adopted. I give a 4 because the clear engineering contributions and released resources offer substantive impact for applied work, while noting the valid concerns about limited theoretical motivation and missing ablations/tuning details that constrain its potential to reshape theoretical understanding or guarantee full reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of lexical entailment in context and provides useful resources (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN) plus empirical evidence that contextualized similarity and the proposed mask-based contextualization improve performance and generalize cross-lingually. However, from a theory-first perspective its impact is limited by the absence of formal motivation connecting the features to semantic notions of entailment and by missing ablations isolating mask statistics versus classifier capacity (logistic regression tuning is not reported). These limitations make the contributions practically useful but unlikely to fundamentally change how researchers conceptualize contextual entailment without further theoretical and analytical follow-up.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important and practical problem—lexical entailment in context—by introducing two reusable datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN) and demonstrating that lightweight context-aware features (masked contextualization over pretrained embeddings) substantially improve performance and even generalize cross-lingually, yielding a new state-of-the-art on an existing benchmark. From my applied multilingual NLP perspective, the combination of simple, interpretable features and cross-lingual evaluation makes the work likely to be adopted and built upon in downstream pipelines and resource creation. While the use of a basic logistic classifier and some missing experimental details limit immediate reproducibility, the empirical gains and released evaluation assets open clear, actionable directions for follow-up work.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early (new datasets, masked contextualization method, cross-lingual generalization), and the writing quality is reported as good. Figures/tables/examples are effective and related work coverage is thorough. However, some presentation shortcomings remain: formal definitions of the masking/statistics operations and exact training details (hyperparameters, classifier tuning) are not reported in the summary, which hampers full clarity and reproducibility. From a theory-first perspective I would have liked slightly more formal notation and explicit definitions around the context masks to match the conceptual claims about entailment. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow and explicitly states the main contributions early (new datasets, masked contextualization method, cross-lingual results). Notation and method descriptions (context masks, feature sets, classifier) are presented consistently and are understandable to a practitioner. Figures/tables and examples (metrics on CONTEXT-PPDB/CONTEXT-WN, baselines, and ablations) are informative, and overall writing quality is good; minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., no hyperparameter reporting or code release mentioned) that could hinder immediate reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early (new datasets, masked contextualization method, cross-lingual generalization), and the writing quality is reported as good. Figures/tables/examples are effective and related work coverage is thorough. However, some presentation shortcomings remain: formal definitions of the masking/statistics operations and exact training details (hyperparameters, classifier tuning) are not reported in the summary, which hampers full clarity and reproducibility. From a theory-first perspective I would have liked slightly more formal notation and explicit definitions around the context masks to match the conceptual claims about entailment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear intro → method → experiments → conclusion flow and explicitly states the main contributions early (new datasets, masked contextualization method, cross-lingual results). Notation and method descriptions (context masks, feature sets, classifier) are presented consistently and are understandable to a practitioner. Figures/tables and examples (metrics on CONTEXT-PPDB/CONTEXT-WN, baselines, and ablations) are informative, and overall writing quality is good; minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., no hyperparameter reporting or code release mentioned) that could hinder immediate reproducibility.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a solid empirical contribution (new datasets, sensible baselines, cross-lingual tests) and achieves strong results, but both also flag missing reproducibility details (hyperparameters/code) and insufficient sensitivity/failure analyses and ablations to isolate the core methodological claim. Because these methodological and reporting gaps materially limit confidence in the novelty and robustness of the proposed mask-based approach, a mid-tier score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides useful empirical evidence (new CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN datasets, cross-lingual tests, and state-of-the-art results vs. context-agnostic baselines) so experiments are broadly adequate. However, it lacks critical ablations and sensitivity analyses that my theoretical perspective requires: there is no thorough isolation of the proposed mask-statistics contribution from classifier capacity (only a logistic regression with no hyperparameter reporting), and key reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are missing. Related work is reported as thorough and limitations are partially discussed, but the absence of targeted failure analyses and formal motivation connecting the mask method to semantic notions of entailment weakens the theoretical grounding.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper provides solid empirical evidence: it introduces two reusable datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN), compares against sensible baselines (including PPDB-specific and context-agnostic baselines), and reports cross-lingual experiments that support the main claims about context masks improving entailment detection. Ablation-style comparisons of masked statistics (min/max/mean) versus simple averaging and Context2Vec alternatives are reported, but the analysis lacks deeper sensitivity checks (e.g., classifier choices, hyperparameter tuning) and more detailed error analysis. Related work is covered thoroughly and some limitations and negative results are acknowledged, however reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s contributions are practically useful but methodologically incremental: it extends existing contextualized representation ideas rather than proposing a new theoretical framing. The addition of two new datasets and cross-lingual evaluation increases its utility and originality beyond a purely incremental engineering tweak, so a score slightly above midpoint is warranted. Therefore I give a 4 to reflect meaningful practical novelty and community value despite modest methodological innovation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a modestly novel engineering contribution—using min/max/mean-derived context masks over pre-trained embeddings and combining word-context similarity features does introduce a new feature design for contextualized lexical entailment—yet the core task (lexical entailment in context) is well-studied. The addition of two datasets and cross-lingual experiments improve evaluation breadth, but the methodological novelty is an incremental extension of existing contextualization and similarity-based approaches rather than a fundamentally new formal treatment of entailment. From a theory-first perspective, the work does not provide a new theoretical framing of entailment, and the use of a simple logistic classifier leaves open whether gains stem from the proposed masks or from feature/classifier interactions, which reduces the perceived originality.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable, practically useful novelty by introducing a masked contextualization technique (min/max/mean over context embeddings) and applying word–context similarity features to lexical entailment in context, which is a meaningful extension beyond context-agnostic baselines. The release of two new datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN) for controlled evaluation strengthens its originality and usefulness for the community. While the modeling approach is relatively simple (logistic regression on engineered features) and builds on existing contextual representation ideas, the clear differentiation from prior PPDB-specific and context-agnostic work and cross-lingual evaluation make the contribution more than purely incremental.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper tackles an important problem, provides two valuable datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN), and achieves strong empirical results with clear relevance to the community. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about limited theoretical grounding for the notion of entailment and missing ablation/hyperparameter details pose minor but important rigor and reproducibility issues, so I award a slightly conservative score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP problem (lexical entailment in context), introduces two new datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN), reports cross-lingual experiments and SOTA results, and uses standard supervised evaluation metrics—all aligning well with a top-tier NLP/ML audience. However, from my theory-first perspective there are concerns about conceptual framing and analysis: claims about ‘entailment’ rely on distributional features without formal semantic motivation, and the methodology omits key ablations and hyperparameter reporting that top venues expect to assess contribution isolation. Given these strengths and the noted analytic gaps, the work is a good fit but has minor scope/rigor concerns for a flagship conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it tackles a core semantic task (lexical entailment in context), introduces two reusable datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN), and establishes a new state-of-the-art with a simple, interpretable method that includes cross-lingual evaluation. The experimental protocol (weighted/macro F1, comparisons to strong baselines) and practical focus on context-aware features align with community expectations and would be of direct interest to researchers and practitioners working on contextual semantics and multilingual applications.

main_claims: Contextualized word representations and word-context similarity features significantly improve detection of lexical entailment in context compared to context-agnostic baselines.

A masked contextualization method (using min/max/mean over context word embeddings to create context masks) outperforms simple averaging and other alternatives for capturing salient context dimensions.
The proposed context-aware features generalize across languages (cross-lingual experiments) and establish a new state-of-the-art on an existing dataset of semantic relations in context.
Two novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN) are introduced for evaluating lexical entailment in context, enabling controlled analysis of sense sensitivity and entailment directionality.
_summary.methodology: Start from pre-trained word-type embeddings (GloVe or bilingual embeddings), compute contextualized word-in-context vectors via a masked filtering approach using statistics (min/max/mean) over context word embeddings, extract word-word and word-context similarity features (including Context2Vec variants), and train a supervised logistic regression classifier to predict entailment.
baselines: Shwartz and Dagan (2015) PPDB-specific features baseline
limitations: Using Context2Vec context representations (~cl,c2v and ~cr,c2v) alone performs poorly (around F1 ≈ 60).

The classifier used is logistic regression with no parameter tuning (except class weights), suggesting exploration of richer features and non-linear classifiers may be needed (mentioned as future work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - detecting lexical entailment in context

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Linguisticist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a research focus on formal semantics and principled model explanations. PhD in linguistics; long track record of work on formal models of lexical semantics and interpretability.
traits: penalises papers that present primarily empirical gains without formal or conceptual analysis of why a method works

requires explicit ablation analyses that isolate contributions of each model component (e.g., mask statistics vs. classifier capacity) and will deduct points if such ablations are missing or incomplete
demands clear theoretical motivation or connection to semantic theory for claims about 'entailment' and will downscore papers that conflate distributional similarity with logical entailment
strict about classifier simplicity vs. feature engineering: will penalize if improvements could be due to increased classifier expressivity rather than proposed features
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I evaluate papers primarily on conceptual clarity and theoretical grounding; strong empirical results must be accompanied by clear, isolated analyses and formal motivation to earn a high score.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Multilingual NLP Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist working on multilingual NLP systems in industry, with experience deploying lexical resources and cross-lingual models. Publishes on applied evaluation and dataset creation for semantics.
traits: rewards papers that introduce well-designed, reusable datasets and clear evaluation protocols, granting substantial credit for release quality and analyses that enable future work
favors practical, empirically-driven advances, especially methods that improve cross-lingual generalization or are lightweight to integrate into existing pipelines
values robust empirical comparisons and will upscore papers that beat strong baselines and establish new state-of-the-art on standard benchmarks

appreciates simple, interpretable methods (e.g., mask statistics + logistic regression) that yield practical gains, and will not penalize the absence of deep theoretical analysis if the empirical contribution is clear
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize empirical significance, dataset contribution, and real-world applicability; practical improvements and reusable resources justify favorable scores even without deep formal justification.
